documents.title: Northeastern University public AI posture sources

ai_strategy.summary: Northeastern presents AI through human-centered research, experiential learning, responsible governance, Khoury AI research, CRAIG, and workforce-oriented AI graduate programs.

key_provisions: Use institutionally reviewed AI tools for sensitive university work.
Protect restricted, confidential, student, personnel, and research data when using AI tools.
Verify AI output and keep humans accountable for academic, administrative, and research decisions.
ai_policy.summary: Northeastern University's public AI policy posture is permissive and emphasizes responsible use, privacy, and human accountability based on reviewed sources.

bodies.name: Center for Responsible AI and Governance
bodies.scope: Northeastern presents AI through human-centered research, experiential learning, responsible governance, Khoury AI research, CRAIG, and workforce-oriented AI graduate programs.

governance_structure.summary: Governance appears distributed across AI-facing offices, centers, technology units, teaching units, or committees; no standalone chief AI officer was confirmed unless named in leadership.

initiatives.description: Northeastern University has public AI guidance, governance, or strategy resources that frame responsible adoption, privacy, academic integrity, and human oversight.
initiatives.significance: This is a concrete R1 signal of AI moving from isolated experimentation toward governed, mission-linked adoption.

regulatory_context.summary: State/system context was checked for MA. Relevant public AI or system governance links are included where found.

narrative_summary: Northeastern University is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is best understood as follows: Northeastern presents AI through human-centered research, experiential learning, responsible governance, Khoury AI research, CRAIG, and workforce-oriented AI graduate programs. The evidence touches governance and policy, teaching and learning, research and innovation, enterprise enablement, student experience, workforce development, and equity or public-impact dimensions. The strongest public sources are first-party AI hubs, guidance pages, research center pages, academic-program materials, and state or system policy resources. Compared with the most centralized AI adopters, several details remain opaque, including named executive AI ownership, exact enterprise user counts, and total AI spending. Even so, the reviewed public record supports a medium-confidence assessment that AI is moving into curriculum, research infrastructure, technology governance, and student or workforce-facing programs rather than remaining purely ad hoc.

gaps_acknowledged: No public chief AI officer was confirmed in reviewed sources.
No total institution-wide AI investment amount was found.
Exact enterprise AI user counts or contract values were usually not public.
Follow-up search closed platform_usage_or_contracts at product/scope level with Claude for Education and Copilot, but no public license cost or active-user count was found.
Follow-up search closed assessment_redesign and named Explorer as GPU-capable HPC, but no consolidated public GPU count was found.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: platform first.
